Title: Processing bodies control the selective translation for optimal development of Arabidopsis young seedlings
Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Precursor of chlorophyll; excessive dark accumulation predisposes seedlings to photobleaching during transition to light.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: DCP5 restrains translation of specific mRNAs in darkness, including protochlorophyllide synthesis enzymes and PILS3, thereby preventing excess protochlorophyllide accumulation and premature apical hook opening; loss of DCP5 causes photobleaching and impaired seedling fitness.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DCP5 is required for p-body formation and selective translational repression in dark-grown seedlings. 2. dcp5-1 shows premature translation of mRNAs encoding protochlorophyllide synthesis enzymes, leading to increased photobleaching risk after illumination. 3. DCP5 also represses translation of PILS3 to maintain proper auxin-dependent apical hook development and optimal skoto-/photomorphogenesis.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Functions in young seedlings, especially in dark-grown and de-etiolating stages
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: P-bodies accumulate in dark-grown seedlings and are reduced upon light exposure
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: dcp5-1 mutant phenotyping; p-body imaging; comparative transcriptome and translatome analyses; dark/light developmental assays; protochlorophyllide/photobleaching phenotypes
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Dark growth, transition to light, soil emergence context
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Comparative transcriptome and translatome analyses; Affymetrix GeneChip microarray (accession NA in provided text)
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of function compromises etiolated and de-etiolating growth, partially open cotyledons under soil, photobleaching, and dwarf stature.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: DCP5-mediated translational buffering is a candidate lever to optimize early seedling vigor and safe emergence, but broad developmental defects make direct disruption undesirable.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: DCP5 is not a metabolic enzyme or transcription factor; it controls nutrient-related metabolism indirectly through selective translational repression of metabolic and developmental mRNAs.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Controls growth programs including apical hook maintenance/opening during seedling emergence.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Premature translation of PILS3 mRNA in dcp5-1 is associated with auxin-dependent apical hook opening defects in dark-grown seedlings.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PILS3 mRNA is a p-body-repressed transcript in darkness. 2. In dcp5-1 seedlings, PILS3 undergoes premature translation. 3. Deregulated PILS3 translation contributes to altered auxin-dependent apical hook opening and reduced seedling fitness during soil emergence.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Prematurely translated in dark-grown dcp5-1 seedlings
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative translatome analysis; mutant developmental phenotyping in dark-grown seedlings
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Misregulated translation may impair apical hook protection, reducing successful soil emergence.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Fine-tuning translational control of PILS3-related auxin partitioning could improve emergence traits, but developmental pleiotropy is likely.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core non-enzyme target because the paper specifically highlights its translational repression by p-bodies in relation to seedling development.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes insertion of Mg2+ into protoporphyrin IX in the chlorophyll biosynthetic pathway, supporting protochlorophyllide formation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Precursor of chlorophyll required for photosynthetic competence; excess accumulation in darkness increases photobleaching/photooxidative risk upon illumination.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Premature translation of GUN5 mRNA in the p-body-defective dcp5-1 mutant contributes to elevated protochlorophyllide accumulation in dark-grown seedlings and increased photobleaching after transfer to light.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GUN5 mRNA is among p-body-repressed transcripts in dark-grown seedlings. 2. In dcp5-1, its translation is prematurely elevated in darkness. 3. Elevated translation is associated with higher protochlorophyllide accumulation and photobleaching susceptibility during de-etiolation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in dark-grown seedlings undergoing skotomorphogenesis/de-etiolation context
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Translated prematurely in dark-grown dcp5-1 seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Translatome/polysome analysis; mutant phenotyping; protochlorophyllide measurement; immunoblot/contextual protein analysis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Dark growth followed by light exposure (de-etiolation)
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptome and translatome analyses; microarray (accession NA in provided text)
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Excess protochlorophyllide in darkness increases photooxidative damage and seedling photobleaching upon illumination.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating translational control of chlorophyll-biosynthetic genes such as GUN5 may improve safe de-etiolation, but overactivation in darkness should be avoided because of photobleaching risk.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper emphasizes translational derepression via loss of DCP5 rather than direct genetic manipulation of GUN5 itself.
